title: Anthropic donates Petri to Meridian Labs and ships version 3.0
subtitle: The open-source alignment-testing tool moves to an independent nonprofit, with a new architecture that separates auditor and target models and brings tests closer to live deployments.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: On May 8, Anthropic announced it is donating Petri — its open-source alignment-testing toolbox — to Meridian Labs, an evaluation-focused nonprofit. The handover ships alongside Petri 3.0, which restructures the tool so the auditor model and the target model are independent components that researchers can swap or tune separately. A new add-on called Dish runs tests using a target's real system prompt and scaffolding, narrowing the gap between Petri's lab conditions and how a model actually behaves once deployed.

Petri tests for behaviors that benchmarks miss: deception, sycophancy, willingness to cooperate with harmful requests, and signs that a model recognizes it is being evaluated. Anthropic has used it on every Claude model since Sonnet 4.5. Moving the project to Meridian mirrors Anthropic's earlier donation of the Model Context Protocol to the Linux Foundation — a recurring pattern of releasing infrastructure once it becomes load-bearing for the wider ecosystem.

The transfer addresses a structural complaint that has dogged AI safety since at least 2024: when the same lab builds the model and the eval, even rigorous results invite skepticism. Regulators in the US Center for AI Standards and Innovation, the EU AI Office, and the UK AI Safety Institute have all said publicly that they prefer evaluations they can run, rerun, and modify. Putting Petri under independent governance is a precondition for that — and signals where the rest of the field's safety tooling is likely to migrate.

For learners: when you read that an AI model 'passed safety testing,' the next question is who designed the test, who ran it, and whether the test scenarios resembled how the model gets used in the real world. Petri 3.0's Dish add-on exists because that last piece — does the test resemble production — has been the weakest link. Independent evaluation infrastructure is unglamorous, but it is what makes any safety claim auditable later.
